original_abstract: The step that cleaves the carbon-halogen bond in copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions remains ill defined because of the multiple redox manifolds available to copper and the instability of the high-valent copper product formed. We report the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitrile to ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes to form previously elusive but here fully characterized copper(III) complexes. The stability of these complexes stems from the strong Cu−CF 3 bond and the high barrier for C( CF 3 )−C( CH 2 CN ) bond-forming reductive elimination. The mechanistic studies we performed suggest that oxidative addition to ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes proceeds by means of two different pathways: an S N 2-type substitution to the ionic complex and a halogen-atom transfer to the neutral complex. We observed a pronounced ligand acceleration of the oxidative addition, which correlates with that observed in the copper-catalyzed couplings of azoles, amines, or alkynes with alkyl electrophiles.
humanized_abstract: The bond-cleaving step in cross-coupling remains poorly characterized due in part to the rich redox chemistry available to copper, and the unknown stability of high-oxidation-state copper species involved. Here we describe the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitrile into ionic copper(I) salts and neutral copper(I) species to generate copper(III) species as active intermediates, previously unknown but fully characterized and studied. These stable species find explanation from the substantial Cu−CF 3 bonding interaction along with high barrier of the corresponding C (CF 3 )−C (CH 2 CN ) bond forming reductive elimination process. For the unknown oxidative-addition step leading to these copper(III) intermediates in the cross-coupling we identified distinct pathways leading to ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes involving an S N 2 process and an atom-transfer reaction to the halogen atom, respectively. Importantly the ligand acceleration (LAE) effect is retained for oxidative addition and is qualitatively similar in magnitude and meaning as that obtained for catalytic couplings of azoles, amines, alkynes or other activations of alkyl halides with alkyl electrophiles.

For the unknown oxidative-addition step leading to these copper(III) intermediates in the cross-coupling we identified distinct pathways leading to ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes involving an S N 2 process and an atom-transfer reaction to the halogen atom, respectively. Importantly the ligand acceleration (LAE) effect is retained for oxidative addition and is qualitatively similar in magnitude and meaning as that obtained for catalytic couplings of azoles, amines, alkynes or other activations of alkyl halides with alkyl electrophiles.
